item.title: Dance
item.description: RYM On Valentine’s Day 1970, David Mancuso needed rent money and threw a party at his loft on 647 Broadway: about a hundred guests, two dollars and fifty cents at the door with food and punch included, a sound system assembled like a hi-fi obsessive’s living room (because it was one), no liquor lic...

item.title: Hip hop
item.description: Periodc. 1973–presentNamed1979 (“Rapper’s Delight” put the syllables on a record; the coinage is conventionally credited to Keef Cowboy, c.

item.title: Soul
item.description: PeriodLate 1950s–presentLocationDetroit, Memphis, Chicago, New York, Muscle ShoalsInfluencesGospelRhythm and bluesDoo-wopJazzLinksRYM Crooner-style horns enter first on “Try a Little Tenderness”: no drums yet, no rhythm section punching at the listener, just Otis Redding’s restrained vocal moving s...

item.title: Cultural omnivorousness
item.description: A philosophy professor who keeps a Dolly Parton record next to the Bach on the shelf would have puzzled the sociologist of 1970.

item.title: The songwriter-performer divide
item.description: The question of who owns the song — the person who wrote it or the person who sings it — runs through the entire history of popular music.
